common_name: Sun Sugar Cherry Tomato (Ultra-Sweet Orange Cherry Tomato from Tokita Seed)

planting.note: Start indoors 6–8 weeks before transplanting; Sun Sugar is an indeterminate cherry tomato requiring staking or caging. In zone 9, transplant February–March for spring production, or September for fall — the long Gulf Coast warm season gives Sun Sugar an extended production window from late spring through early summer (and again in fall). Sun Sugar has exceptional crack resistance compared to similar orange cherry types — the thin-skinned fruits resist the longitudinal cracking that ruins many cherry tomatoes after rain. Harvest when fruit is deep golden-orange and gives slightly to gentle squeeze; a slight translucency appears at full ripeness.

directions: Start seeds indoors at 75–80°F; transplant after hardening off. Cage or stake immediately — vines reach 5–6 feet. Harvest regularly to encourage continued production. Sun Sugar is a Japanese F1 hybrid cherry tomato developed by Tokita Seed Company and introduced to the American market in the 1990s — one of the most widely planted and beloved cherry tomatoes in home gardens for its combination of extraordinary sweetness, complex flavor, and reliable productivity. The key characteristic of Sun Sugar is its Brix measurement: at 14+ Brix, Sun Sugar regularly exceeds the sweetness of any standard garden tomato and approaches the sugar concentration of some dessert fruits. The flavor is not merely sweet but layered — a tropical, almost apricot-melon complexity underlies the sweetness, creating a flavor profile that distinguishes Sun Sugar from flatter sweet cherry types. The color is a distinctive deep golden-orange at ripeness, clearly different from the yellow-orange of varieties like Sun Gold, and the round, 0.75–1 inch fruits are borne in trusses of 15–30 on each cluster, providing heavy production throughout the season. Sun Sugar is grown by gardeners who want the maximum flavor reward per cherry tomato plant in the smallest garden space.
sources: Tokita Seed Company — Sun Sugar cherry tomato F1 hybrid introduction
Johnny's Selected Seeds — Cherry tomato Brix and variety comparison
University of California Cooperative Extension — Specialty Cherry Tomato Variety Guide

common_name: Bright Lights Swiss Chard (AAS-Winning Rainbow Multi-Color Stem Swiss Chard)

planting.note: Direct sow or start indoors 4–6 weeks before transplanting; Bright Lights is a cool-season biennial treated as an annual. In zone 9, sow September–November for fall and winter production, or February–March for spring. Swiss chard tolerates light frost but is damaged by hard freezes — in zone 9, plants grown through the mild winter provide harvest from fall through spring. Bright Lights produces a mix of stem colors (yellow, orange, red, pink, white, purple, and intermediate shades) within each packet, creating a visually striking garden planting and a colorful harvest of mixed-stem bunches.

directions: Direct sow 0.5 inch deep; each chard 'seed' is actually a seed cluster that may produce 2–3 seedlings — thin to the strongest. Harvest outer leaves at any size; plants continue producing from the center. Bright Lights is a 1998 All-America Selections-winning Swiss chard — a variety selection that produces the same flavor and productivity as standard chard while dramatically expanding the visual appeal of the garden. Each individual plant in a Bright Lights planting develops stems of a single color, but a planting from any seed packet includes plants with yellow, orange, red, pink, white, lavender, and purple stems, with the blade of each leaf complementarily colored against the bright stem. Photographed in morning light, a mature Bright Lights planting is among the most colorful edible garden plantings available in any vegetable category. The flavor is identical to standard Swiss chard — the young leaves are mild and spinach-like for raw salads; the mature leaves are substantial and slightly earthy-sweet when cooked. The colored stems and midribs hold their color reasonably well through cooking (yellow and white stems hold best; red and pink stems bleed somewhat). Bright Lights is valued in both edible gardens and ornamental-edible plantings where it provides extended color through the cooler months.
sources: All-America Selections — Bright Lights Swiss chard AAS winner 1998
Johnny's Selected Seeds — Chard variety comparison
Cornell Cooperative Extension — Swiss Chard Production Guide

common_name: Ruby Red Swiss Chard (Classic Deep-Red Stem Rhubarb Chard)

planting.note: Direct sow or start indoors 4–6 weeks before transplanting; in zone 9, sow September–November for fall and winter harvest, or February–March for spring. Ruby Red is one of the most cold-tolerant chard varieties, surviving light freezes and tolerating temperatures into the mid-20s°F in established plantings. The intense red stem color develops most vividly in cool fall and winter growing conditions — heat reduces the pigmentation intensity. Begin harvesting outer leaves once plants are 6–8 inches tall; regular harvest encourages continued production from the growing center.

directions: Direct sow 0.5 inch deep; thin to 9–12 inches. Harvest outer leaves regularly, leaving the growing center intact. Ruby Red Swiss Chard (also called Rhubarb Chard for the resemblance of the brilliant crimson stems to culinary rhubarb stalks) is the original and defining red-stemmed chard — the variety from which all colored-stem selections and rainbow chard blends derive. Ruby Red produces large, dark green, heavily crinkled and savoyed leaves on vivid crimson to ruby-red stems and midribs — a color combination that makes the plant one of the most ornamentally striking vegetables in the cool-season garden, visually dramatic against the dark green leaf tissue. The crimson pigment in Ruby Red's stems is betacyanin — the same class of pigment found in red beets (Beta vulgaris, the same genus) — a natural antioxidant with deep red to violet-red coloration. Ruby Red has longer American cultivation history than most colored chard types, appearing in American seed catalogs under 'Rhubarb Chard' nomenclature for well over a century. The flavor is classic chard — mild and slightly earthy-sweet when young, more substantial and beet-adjacent in mature leaves. The red stem color is retained reasonably well through steaming but bleeds in boiling water; roasting or sautéing in fat preserves the best color.
sources: Seed Savers Exchange — Ruby Red chard / Rhubarb chard variety history
Baker Creek Heirloom Seeds — Rhubarb chard variety notes
Cornell Cooperative Extension — Swiss Chard Variety Evaluation

common_name: Lipstick Pimento Pepper (Small Sweet Heart-Shaped Thick-Walled Red Pepper)

planting.note: Start indoors 6–8 weeks before transplanting; Lipstick produces compact plants (18–24 inch) covered with small, heart-shaped sweet peppers that mature from green to brilliant red. In zone 9, transplant March–April for summer and fall production. The thick, sweet walls make Lipstick excellent for fresh eating at any stage (green or red), for roasting and peeling as a pimento-type pepper, for stuffing (the blunt, rounded cavity is ideal for stuffing whole), and for drying as a sweet dried pepper. Heat level: essentially zero — among the mildest sweet peppers grown.

directions: Start seeds indoors at 78–85°F; transplant after last frost. Compact plants set heavily in warm weather — no staking needed. Lipstick is a pimento-class sweet pepper — a member of the heart-shaped, very thick-walled, extremely sweet-fleshed pepper category that differs fundamentally from bell peppers in form, wall thickness, and sugar content. Pimento peppers have significantly thicker walls than bell peppers (the flesh-to-cavity ratio is much higher), essentially no heat, much higher sweetness, and a distinctive tapered, blunt, heart-shaped form rather than the blocky, squared bell shape. Lipstick produces 2–3 inch, heart-shaped peppers — smaller than full-sized pimento types — on compact plants that set fruit very heavily and continuously through the warm season. The red-ripe fruits have a sweet, clean pepper flavor without any of the slight bitterness that can appear in thick-walled bell peppers, making them excellent for eating raw at full ripeness or briefly roasted. The small size makes them ideal for eating whole as a snack pepper, for miniature stuffed preparations, and for the concentrated pimento flavor that goes into pimento cheese — one of the classic Southern condiment spreads for which pimento peppers are the defining ingredient.
sources: Harris Seeds — Lipstick pimento pepper variety notes
Johnny's Selected Seeds — Pimento and specialty sweet pepper variety comparison
North Carolina State University Extension — Sweet Pepper Production Guide

common_name: Powder Blue Rabbiteye Blueberry (Late-Season Heavy-Bearing Gulf South Blueberry)

planting.note: Plant nursery-grown rooted cuttings in fall or spring in acidic (pH 4.5–5.5) soil amended with generous amounts of pine bark, peat moss, or acidic compost; rabbiteye blueberries are among the most heat and drought-tolerant blueberries for zone 7–9. Powder Blue requires cross-pollination from a second rabbiteye variety (Brightwell, Tifblue, Premier, or Climax) for good fruit set; plant at least two different rabbiteye varieties. Mulch heavily with pine bark or pine straw. Powder Blue is a late-season rabbiteye ripening in July in the Gulf South — after earlier varieties like Climax and Premier — extending the total blueberry harvest window by 3–4 weeks.

directions: Plant in acidic, well-drained, organically rich soil at the same depth as the nursery container; mulch 4–6 inches deep with pine bark or pine straw to maintain soil acidity and moisture. Do not fertilize with standard garden fertilizers — use acid-specific fertilizers (ammonium sulfate or azalea/camellia formulas). Powder Blue is a rabbiteye blueberry (Vaccinium virgatum, formerly classified as V. ashei) released in 1978 by North Carolina State University — one of the most widely planted and highest-yielding rabbiteye varieties across the Southeast. Rabbiteye blueberries are the blueberry species native to the Gulf South — the wild Vaccinium virgatum grows naturally throughout the coastal plain from North Carolina to Florida and west to Louisiana — and they are dramatically better adapted to the heat, humidity, and acidic sandy soils of the Gulf South than the northern highbush blueberries that dominate the commercial blueberry market. Powder Blue produces very large berries for a rabbiteye — medium-firm, light blue (giving the variety its name), with a mild, sweet flavor. The late-season ripening (July in zone 8–9) means Powder Blue fills the end of the Gulf South blueberry season after earlier-ripening varieties have peaked, extending the household fresh blueberry window into midsummer. Established rabbiteye plantings (plants reach bearing age in 3–5 years and can remain productive for 30–50 years) are highly cost-effective long-term investments for Gulf South home fruit gardens.
sources: North Carolina State University Extension — Powder Blue rabbiteye blueberry release notes
University of Florida IFAS Extension — Blueberry Varieties for Florida
Louisiana State University AgCenter — Blueberry Production in Louisiana
